methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
ACSL is a public-stage company in the Drones & Autonomous sector, listed on the Tokyo exchange. The comparable universe consists of five private peers—Joby Aviation, Nuro, Archer Aviation, Elroy Air, and Terra Drone—with no public-stage peers identified in the same sector. This composition reflects the nascent state of the Drones & Autonomous market, where public listings remain sparse.

The peer-median valuation method is not applicable to this analysis. The four same-sector peers identified failed to meet the minimum threshold of three datapoints when filtered by stage alignment, adjacent-stage proximity, or valuation-tier similarity. Applying an all-sector median would introduce material distortion given the heterogeneity of drone and autonomous systems businesses across applications, commercialization timelines, and unit economics. As a result, no triangulated valuation range can be derived from comparable-company analysis using the data provided.

This analysis is constrained by structural limitations in the Drones & Autonomous peer set. A valuation conclusion would require either: (1) expansion to adjacent subsectors with sufficient public or late-stage private comparables; (2) application of alternative methods such as DCF or precedent transaction analysis; or (3) accumulation of additional peer data as the sector matures. The scarcity of tradeable public comps underscores the stage risk and information asymmetry inherent in early-market autonomous systems companies.
